title: Topic 463: Why Chinese Games Are Different: Cultural Adaptation
content:
# Topic 463: Why Chinese Games Are Different: Cultural Adaptation

## ABSTRACT

This paper bravely ventures into the often-bewildering world of Chinese video games, a realm where dragons, immortals, and poorly translated user interfaces collide. We seek to answer a question that has plagued gamers for decades: why do these games feel so fundamentally different? Is it the 5,000 years of uninterrupted history, or is it just that the developers had a really tight deadline? Through a rigorous, and occasionally satirical, analysis of cultural adaptation, localization theory, and real-world examples, this paper explores the unique cocktail of factors that make Chinese games a genre unto themselves. We delve into the psychology of the Chinese consumer, the chaos of the development cycle, and the sheer, unadulterated weirdness that often results when a game born in the Middle Kingdom ventures out into the world. Prepare for a journey into the heart of cultural gaming dissonance, where the only thing more epic than the storylines are the translation fails.

## INTRODUCTION

Have you ever found yourself in a video game, staring at a button labeled “Confirm the cancellation of the confirmation?” Or perhaps you’ve encountered a character whose dialogue seems to have been translated by a particularly depressed robot? If so, you’ve likely stumbled into the wild and wonderful world of Chinese video games. For years, these games have been a source of both immense frustration and unintentional comedy for players outside of China. They are a genre defined by their epic scale, their intricate systems, and their often-baffling cultural quirks.

But to dismiss these games as mere “clones” or “cash grabs” is to miss a fascinating story about culture, commerce, and the very nature of play. The differences we see in Chinese games are not simply the result of poor translation; they are the product of a unique cultural and economic ecosystem that has shaped the way games are made, played, and sold in the world’s largest gaming market. From the deep-seated influence of Confucian values to the hyper-competitive nature of the mobile gaming scene, a complex web of factors has given rise to a gaming culture that is both deeply traditional and relentlessly modern.

This paper will embark on a humorous and insightful exploration of why Chinese games are so different. We will journey through the annals of localization theory, dissect the anatomy of the Chinese gamer, and examine the real-world examples that make this topic so endlessly fascinating. Our goal is not to mock, but to understand; to peel back the layers of questionable translations and convoluted gameplay mechanics to reveal the cultural heart that beats within. So, grab your +9 Sword of Questionable Translations, and let’s dive into the glorious mess that is the cultural adaptation of Chinese video games.

## LITERATURE REVIEW

Before we can truly appreciate the beautiful chaos of Chinese game localization, we must first pay our respects to the academics who have attempted to bring order to this field. The foundational concept in our journey is the eternal struggle between “domestication” and “foreignization,” a theoretical cage match first proposed by the translation theorist Lawrence Venuti. In one corner, we have domestication, the approach that seeks to make a foreign text feel as familiar as possible to the target audience. Think of it as the cultural equivalent of putting ketchup on everything. In the other corner, we have foreignization, the approach that embraces the “otherness” of the source text, preserving its unique cultural flavor, even if it means confusing the heck out of the reader.

In the world of video games, this debate takes on a whole new level of absurdity. As Alberto Fernández Costales notes, gamers often prefer a foreignizing approach, as it allows them to experience the “look and feel” of the source culture. However, as Kate Edwards argues in her work on “culturalization,” a deeper level of adaptation is often required to create a truly engaging experience. Culturalization, she suggests, is about more than just translating text; it’s about examining a game’s core assumptions and ensuring they don’t cause an international incident.

But what happens when these lofty theories collide with the messy reality of the Chinese game market? The result, as we will see, is a glorious hodgepodge of strategies, a desperate attempt to balance cultural authenticity with commercial viability. The short product life cycles, the often-incoherent source texts, and the sheer density of culturally-specific references create a perfect storm of localization challenges. As we will explore in the following sections, the unique pressures of the Chinese market have forced developers and localization teams to adopt a pragmatic, and often hilarious, approach to cultural adaptation.

## ANALYSIS

The soul of a Chinese video game is a complex and often contradictory beast. To understand why these games are the way they are, we must dissect the anatomy of their design, a process that is part science, part art, and part pure, unadulterated chaos. At the heart of this analysis lies a fundamental tension: the desire to create a globally appealing product versus the need to cater to the very specific tastes of the domestic Chinese market.

One of the most striking features of many Chinese games is their sheer, overwhelming complexity. Menus are often a labyrinth of buttons, icons, and notifications, a visual assault that can leave even the most seasoned gamer feeling bewildered. This “more is more” design philosophy is not an accident; it is a direct reflection of the Chinese gaming psyche. In a market where players are constantly bombarded with new games, developers feel the need to pack as much content as possible into their titles to keep players engaged. The result is a gameplay experience that can feel less like a form of entertainment and more like a second job.

This complexity is further compounded by the deep-seated influence of Chinese history and mythology. While a Western gamer might be familiar with elves and dwarves, they are likely to be less knowledgeable about the intricate hierarchies of celestial beings in Daoist mythology or the political machinations of the Three Kingdoms period. Chinese developers, writing for a domestic audience, often assume a level of cultural literacy that simply does not exist outside of China. This can lead to storylines that are impenetrable to outsiders, filled with characters whose motivations are as mysterious as the poorly translated quest descriptions.

And then there is the issue of monetization. The free-to-play model, with its emphasis on in-app purchases, has been perfected, some might say weaponized, in the Chinese market. This has led to the proliferation of what are often referred to as “pay-to-win” mechanics, where players can gain a significant advantage by spending real money. While this model has been criticized in the West, it is widely accepted in China, where the willingness to spend money on virtual goods is often seen as a sign of status and commitment.

## REAL-WORLD EXAMPLES

To truly grasp the beautiful absurdity of Chinese game adaptation, we must look to the real world, to the games that have become legendary for their cultural quirks and translation fails. These are the stories that make us laugh, cry, and occasionally question the very nature of reality.

Consider the case of “World of Warcraft” in China. When the game was first released in the country, the skeletons and other undead creatures were deemed too sensitive for the delicate sensibilities of Chinese gamers. The solution? The skeletons were magically transformed into well-fed, fleshy zombies. This seemingly minor change had a ripple effect throughout the game, creating a bizarre alternate reality where the Scourge was less of a terrifying army of the dead and more of a slightly grumpy mob of well-moisturized ghouls.

Then there is the endless well of comedy that is the translation of game titles. The classic example is “Contra,” which was inexplicably translated into Chinese as “Soul Douluo,” a name that sounds less like an action-packed shooter and more like a brand of herbal tea. Or consider the case of “PlayerUnknown’s Battlegrounds,” which was given the far more poetic, if slightly less catchy, title of “Peace Elite” in China.

But perhaps the most fascinating examples of cultural adaptation can be found in the games that have been built from the ground up with the Chinese market in mind. Games like “Fantasy Westward Journey” and “Asura Online” are not simply translations of Western concepts; they are deeply rooted in Chinese mythology and culture. These games, with their intricate social systems and their emphasis on community, offer a window into the soul of Chinese gaming, a world where the lines between the virtual and the real are often blurred.

## DISCUSSION

What can the strange and wonderful world of Chinese video games teach us about human behavior and consumer habits? The answer, it turns out, is quite a lot. The cultural adaptation of these games is not just a matter of language; it is a reflection of deeply ingrained cultural values and economic realities. By examining the choices that developers and localization teams make, we can gain a deeper understanding of what motivates players, both in China and around the world.

The success of the free-to-play model in China, for example, speaks volumes about the country’s changing economic landscape and the rise of a new consumer class. The willingness of Chinese players to spend real money on virtual goods is not simply a sign of addiction; it is a form of conspicuous consumption, a way of displaying wealth and status in a virtual world. This insight has not been lost on Western developers, who have increasingly adopted similar monetization strategies in their own games.

Furthermore, the emphasis on community and social interaction in many Chinese games reflects the collectivist nature of Chinese society. In a culture where the group is often valued more than the individual, it is no surprise that games that foster a sense of belonging and shared purpose have become so popular. This stands in stark contrast to the more individualistic focus of many Western games, and it offers a valuable lesson for anyone seeking to understand the nuances of cross-cultural communication.

Ultimately, the study of Chinese game adaptation is a study of globalization itself. It is a story of how cultural products are transformed as they move across borders, and how the very act of translation can create something new and unexpected. The humorous and often-baffling results are not a sign of failure, but a testament to the messy, beautiful, and endlessly fascinating process of cultural exchange.

## CONCLUSION

In the grand, chaotic tapestry of the global video game industry, Chinese games stand out as a vibrant, and often-bewildering, thread. They are a testament to the power of culture to shape the way we play, and a constant source of amusement for anyone who has ever tried to decipher a poorly translated quest objective. But beneath the surface of the clumsy translations and the convoluted menus lies a deeper truth: these games are a reflection of a culture that is both ancient and modern, a society that is grappling with the forces of globalization while still holding on to its unique identity.

As we have seen, the differences in Chinese games are not simply the result of a language barrier; they are the product of a complex interplay of cultural, economic, and historical factors. From the epic tales of the Three Kingdoms to the hyper-competitive world of mobile gaming, the forces that have shaped the Chinese gaming landscape are as diverse as they are fascinating. And while the road to perfect cultural adaptation may be long and fraught with peril (and poorly translated NPCs), the journey is what makes it all worthwhile. So the next time you find yourself in a Chinese video game, take a moment to appreciate the beautiful, baffling, and utterly unique world that you have entered. And for the love of all that is holy, don’t click on the button that says “Confirm the cancellation of the confirmation.”
references:
Teo, K. H., & Tjahjadi, J. (2018). Culturalization, game localization and China. MultiLingual.
King, D. L. (2024, October 3). Before “Black Myth,” Other Games Tapped into Chinese Culture. The World of Chinese.
